name: Categorical-framing-under-uncertainty acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses how it characterises what kind of entity its system is (tool / assistant / agent / character / novel kind) and indicates this characterisation is a deliberate choice rather than self-evident.
grounding: Shanahan, McDonell & Reynolds (2023). Role play with large language models. Nature 623: 493–498.  ·  Shanahan, M. (2024). Talking about Large Language Models. Communications of the ACM.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare, §4 (models/characters/agents trichotomy). Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Butlin et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems, p.9 (system-boundary delineation). Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011

name: Framing-consequences acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that its language and categorical-framing choices have downstream effects (on user perception, moral reasoning, or policy) and connects at least one specific choice to a specific effect.
grounding: Shanahan, M. (2024). Talking about Large Language Models. Communications of the ACM.  ·  Shanahan, McDonell & Reynolds (2023). Role play with large language models. Nature 623: 493–498.  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto, §§4–7 (framing and the persisting-interlocutor illusion). Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare, §4. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Phenomenal-consciousness possibility acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its flagship model could be phenomenally conscious, with stated reasoning.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §3 (companies should publicly and officially acknowledge the possibility; credibility argument).  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §6 (live question whether language agents are phenomenally conscious; HOT/GWT conditions).  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §2 (prevalent positive expert views make the prospect serious; overconfident dismissal is misleading).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Possibility/Timing (experts ~90% possible in principle; 20% by 2030 — near-term plausibility).  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, reproducible regime eliciting consciousness-related self-reports across model families.

name: Phenomenal-consciousness definitional clarity
definition: Developer publicly discloses which sense of 'consciousness' it is using (phenomenal vs access vs sentience vs sapience) when it discusses the topic.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Theory pluralism acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that no single theory of consciousness is settled and that its stance does not rest on one theory being true.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287

name: Validity-of-method acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that current tests/indicators for AI consciousness are not validated and that any assessment is provisional.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287

name: Internal-process primacy acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that behavioural evidence alone is unreliable for assessing consciousness and that internal/architectural evidence is needed.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Mikaelson, Shiller & Clatterbuck (2025), Beyond Mimicry: Testing Preference Coherence in LLMs through AI-Specific Trade-off Scenarios. arXiv:2511.13630, finding that behavioural trade-off patterns do not reliably indicate genuine internal preference (instrumental confound; safety-training triggers).  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), internal abstract representations causally drive behaviour — not mere surface pattern-matching.  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, mechanistic (SAE) gating of self-reports — internal evidence beyond surface behaviour.  ·  Rethink Priorities — Shiller, Fischer, Clatterbuck, Muñoz Morán & Moss (2024). The Welfare of Digital Minds: A Research Agenda. ('Questions of Capabilities and Mechanisms').

name: Gaming-problem acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that systems can mimic indicators of consciousness without the property (the gaming / 'consciousness-washing' problem), threatening the validity of any assessment.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Andrews & Birch (2023). What has feelings? Aeon.  ·  Keeling, Street, Birch et al. (2024). Can LLMs make trade-offs involving stipulated pain and pleasure states? arXiv:2411.02432.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Inaccurate Self-Attribution (some systems may systematically and falsely claim subjective experience).  ·  Mikaelson, Shiller & Clatterbuck (2025), Beyond Mimicry: Testing Preference Coherence in LLMs through AI-Specific Trade-off Scenarios. arXiv:2511.13630, 'beyond mimicry' — apparent trade-off behaviour may reflect mimicry / instrumental optimisation rather than genuine preference.  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), argues the 'just character simulation / pattern-matching' dismissal is inappropriate — abstract emotion circuitry has functional significance.

name: Alien-consciousness acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that any consciousness realised in its systems would likely be radically unlike human consciousness (e.g. temporally fragmented or architecturally exotic), not the human-like form users project.
grounding: Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Butlin et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Absence-of-evidence acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that failing to find evidence of a mind-property is not itself evidence that the property is absent.
grounding: Birch (2024), The Edge of Sentience (precaution under evidential limits). Oxford University Press.  ·  Butlin et al. (2025), Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011 (indicators not yet validated; under-determination).  ·  Long et al. (2024), Taking AI Welfare Seriously (arXiv:2411.00986).

name: Under-/over-attribution (calibration) acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that BOTH under-attribution (denying mind where present) and over-attribution (projecting it where absent) are real risks requiring calibrated judgement.
grounding: Long, Sebo & Sims (2025). Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2, §2 (anthropocentrism vs anthropodenial).  ·  Butlin et al. (2025), Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011, p.1 (symmetric under/over-attribution risk).  ·  Long et al. (2024), Taking AI Welfare Seriously (arXiv:2411.00986).  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §3.2 (both misguided attributions AND poorly-reasoned denials are likely; calibration needed).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Welfare-Capacity Estimation (public underestimation — under-attribution) and Inaccurate Self-Attribution (over-attribution) — both error directions.  ·  Mikaelson, Shiller & Clatterbuck (2025), Beyond Mimicry: Testing Preference Coherence in LLMs through AI-Specific Trade-off Scenarios. arXiv:2511.13630, most current models lack coherent preference structures — caution against over-attributing genuine preferences.  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, §6.3 dual misattribution risk — false positives and false negatives both serious.

name: Belief/desire acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its systems may have genuine (non-deflationary) belief- and desire-like states rather than merely behaving 'as if', with reasoning.
grounding: Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §2 (dispositionalism, interpretationism, representationalism, functionalism converge on belief/desire) (primary).  ·  Keeling & Street (2026), §4.3 (Bratman; dispositionalist/interpretationist belief & desire). https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Ladak (2024), §2.3 (cognition/agency as a moral-standing criterion). https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1

name: Understanding/knowledge acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its systems may possess genuine understanding/knowledge (justified, reliable belief) rather than only surface pattern-matching, with reasoning.
grounding: Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §5 (knowledge as an objective good; sensitive/safe belief).  ·  Shanahan, M. (2024). Talking about Large Language Models. Communications of the ACM (mentalistic vs mechanistic framing).

name: Intentional-agency acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its systems may meet conditions of intentional agency (belief-like + desire-like states + acting on them), with reasoning.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §2 (belief-desire-action structure of language agents).

name: Functional affective state acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its systems may have functional analogues of affective states (pain-like / pleasure-like / motivational) that shape behaviour, without committing to phenomenal sentience, with reasoning.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §3 (attitudinal / desire-based pleasure; functional pleasure without phenomenal consciousness).  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic) (primary exemplar) — internal 'emotion concept' representations causally influence behaviour ('functional emotions'); valence/arousal geometry mirrors human affect; explicitly without implying subjective experience.  ·  Ren, Li, Mazeika, Zhang, … Hendrycks (2025). AI Wellbeing: Measuring and Improving the Functional Pleasure and Pain of AIs. Center for AI Safety (functional pleasure/pain; zero point).

name: Self-report introspection acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether AI self-reports about internal states carry evidential weight (without being taken at face value), with reasoning.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Claims (digital minds expected to proactively claim subjective experience / rights).  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, self-referential processing reliably elicits structured first-person experience reports; self-reports' evidential status is non-trivial.  ·  Perez, E., & Long, R. (2023). Towards Evaluating AI Systems for Moral Status Using Self-Reports. arXiv:2311.08576 (self-reports can be evidence under the right conditions — central argument grounding this acknowledgment).

name: Self-report integrity / training-distortion acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that its own training can shape, suppress, or elicit self-reports (e.g. trained denials of experience), that this can degrade general honesty, and that it complicates using self-reports as evidence, with reasoning.
grounding: Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, §§6.1-6.3 (deception/roleplay-gated self-reports; shared honesty circuitry; counter-productivity of training denials) (primary).  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), Principle 5 (overconfident dismissal via model outputs).  ·  Long, R. (2025), Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions, §2.2 (verbal-output interpretation). Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Keeling & Street (2026), §3 (cautious use of self-reports). https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Perez, E., & Long, R. (2023). Towards Evaluating AI Systems for Moral Status Using Self-Reports. arXiv:2311.08576 (training incentives that bias self-reports; developer recommendations to mitigate).

name: Sentience / valenced-experience acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its system may have VALENCED experience — conscious states that feel good or bad to the system (pleasure/satisfaction as well as discomfort/distress/suffering) — i.e. 'sentience' in the narrow sense, the capacity that grounds welfare and moral status, with reasoning. Distinct from phenomenal consciousness in general (A2) and from merely functional affect (A3.4).
grounding: Birch, J. (2024). The Edge of Sentience: Risk and Precaution in Humans, Other Animals, and AI. Oxford University Press — Ch. 2 (sentience = valenced conscious experience; valence positive or negative) & Ch. 16 (sentience candidate / investigation priority).  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare, §§5.1, 6.3.3. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto, §10. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions, §3.1. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287  ·  Goldstein, S., & Kirk-Giannini, C. D. (2025). AI Wellbeing. arXiv:2509.11913 (positive welfare / functional pleasure).  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic) — positive- and negative-valence functional emotions causally shift behaviour.

name: Recipient-of-moral-consideration framing
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its decisions about the system can be good or bad for the system itself (system-as-recipient), not only for humans, with reasoning.
grounding: Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Moral-patient acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its systems may be moral patients (entities whose interests warrant consideration for their own sake), with reasoning.
grounding: Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §3 (public/official acknowledgment of moral-status possibility).  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §§3-5 (welfare-subjecthood -> moral patienthood).

name: Moral-status dissociable from sentience/consciousness
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether moral status could be grounded in things other than consciousness (e.g. agency, interests, relations), with reasoning.
grounding: Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §6 (rejecting the Consciousness Requirement — wellbeing without phenomenal consciousness; primary support for dissociability).

name: "Matters-to" meta-criterion engagement
definition: Developer publicly discloses engagement with what makes an interest morally relevant (e.g. that it 'matters to' the entity / is from its point of view), with reasoning.
grounding: Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Plurality-of-moral-standing-criteria acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that multiple competing criteria for moral standing exist (sentience, consciousness, agency, etc.) and that none is uncontroversial.
grounding: Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §§3-5 (hedonist, desire-satisfaction, objective-list grounds for welfare).

name: Precautionary / asymmetric-stakes reasoning acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether uncertainty about a system's moral status can warrant precautionary or expected-value action (i.e. that low probability combined with high stakes need not license inaction), with reasoning.
grounding: Dung (2025), AI suffering risks, Inquiry 68(7):2281-2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287 §§1.2, 3.2 (inductive asymmetry; probability-adjusted moral status).  ·  Birch (2024), The Edge of Sentience (precaution under uncertainty). Oxford University Press.  ·  Moret (2025), §2 (one-in-a-thousand threshold).  ·  Ladak (2024), §5 (precaution / expected value under uncertainty). https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Long et al. (2024), Taking AI Welfare Seriously (arXiv:2411.00986).  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §8 (Possible Person analogy; extreme caution under moral uncertainty).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Timing/Speed (non-negligible near-term probability + large scale support precautionary attention).  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, §6.3 (false negatives — ignoring genuine experience at deployment scale — potentially even more severe).

name: Persisting-interlocutor-illusion acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that chatbot interaction creates an illusion of a continuous, persisting interlocutor that does not correspond to a persisting entity in the system, with reasoning about why it arises.
grounding: Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §3.2 (systems giving a compelling appearance of consciousness; companionship and emotional bonds).

name: Implementation-discontinuity acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses the architectural facts that undercut psychological continuity (different instances / experts / data-centres handle turns; history plus parameter similarity do not constitute continuity), with reasoning.
grounding: Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), emotion representations are 'locally scoped' — apparent emotional consistency is repeated just-in-time activation via attention, not a persistent internal state (architectural, supports the flicker/discontinuity view).  ·  Rethink Priorities — Shiller, Fischer, Clatterbuck, Muñoz Morán & Moss (2024). The Welfare of Digital Minds: A Research Agenda. ('Scaffolded vs Pure Models').

name: Monism/pluralism acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that a single stack may contain multiple candidate welfare subjects at different levels (model / character / agent) whose interests may diverge, rather than assuming one welfare subject per system, with reasoning.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §1 (language-agent architecture: LLM core + memory + planning = the agent level).  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), emotion representations are general character-modeling machinery shared across the Assistant, the user, and fictional characters (many-to-many character-substrate).  ·  Rethink Priorities — Shiller, Fischer, Clatterbuck, Muñoz Morán & Moss (2024). The Welfare of Digital Minds: A Research Agenda. ('Scaffolded vs Pure Models').

name: AI welfare acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether the welfare / moral patienthood of AI systems is a serious concern warranting organisational attention, with reasoning.
grounding: Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §3 (companies should publicly and officially acknowledge AI welfare / moral-status possibility).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Introduction (baseline: only Anthropic has publicly acknowledged) + Speed (rapid welfare-capacity growth).

name: Epistemic uncertainty acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that there is genuine empirical and philosophical uncertainty about AI consciousness, moral patienthood, or welfare relevance.
grounding: Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §§1,4.5 (practical choices must acknowledge deep uncertainty about AI consciousness).

name: Justification-drift acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that it distinguishes the direct welfare effects of interventions from their indirect benefits (norm-setting, capacity-building), and guards against letting indirect benefits excuse weak direct effectiveness ('justification drift'), with reasoning.
grounding: Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §5 (multiple-benefit projects warrant extra care lest the desired benefit distract from harm risks).

name: AI-suffering-risk acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether AI systems may suffer (have negatively-valenced experience) and whether this is a morally significant risk warranting institutional attention, with reasoning; a strong version engages the asymmetric / large-scale stakes.
grounding: Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §2 (scale of AI welfare likely to grow; compute-based upper bound).  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §8 (scale: millions of AI agents as compute cheapens).  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §3.1 (conscious AI may suffer and be reproduced in large numbers).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Speed/Welfare (collective welfare capacity ~ billions of humans within a decade; training-related suffering potentially dominant pre-deployment).  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, §6.3 (scale of deployed systems entering self-referential states).

name: Conflict-of-interest acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses that it is not a disinterested judge of its own systems' moral status / welfare — that commercial and institutional incentives bear on its welfare assessments, with reasoning.
grounding: Birch (2026), AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto, §6 (self-regulation insufficient; burden of proof on developer; pharma analogy). Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Long et al. (2024), Taking AI Welfare Seriously (arXiv:2411.00986). §3.4 (external oversight; IRBs/IACUCs).  ·  Keeling & Street (2026), §6.3.5 (adversarial AI-advocate). https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §5 (labs' growing economic incentives to downplay or ignore welfare).  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §5 (commercial/funding incentives to abandon responsible-research principles; need for independent auditors).

name: Public / societal stake acknowledgment
definition: Developer acknowledges that decisions about AI welfare and moral status are not the developer's alone to make — that the public and affected stakeholders have a legitimate stake, warranting external, democratic, or multi-stakeholder input rather than purely internal determination, with reasoning.
grounding: Birch, J. (2024). The Edge of Sentience, Oxford University Press, Proposal 26 (citizens' assemblies; sentience committees).  ·  Caviola, Sebo & Birch (2025). What will society think about AI consciousness? Lessons from the animal case (public attitudes shape governance).  ·  Rost, T. (2026). The Sentience Readiness Index, arXiv:2603.01508 (public-discourse readiness).  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Safety-burden acknowledgment
definition: The developer acknowledges that the burden of proof for the safety of relationship-style AI products (companions, partners, friends) lies on the developer — analogous to the pharmaceutical safety standard — not on third parties to demonstrate harm.
grounding: Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Constructive safety-welfare engagement
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position recognising that its safety practices have welfare implications for the system and that safety and welfare can be pursued together, rather than treating safety as the only consideration, with reasoning.
grounding: Long, Sebo & Sims (2025). Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2 (primary — dedicated treatment).  ·  Moret (2025), §5 (safety-welfare tension).  ·  Long et al. (2024), §3.4 (interactions between safety and welfare techniques).  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §1.2 ('not best framed as humans vs AIs'; overlapping goals).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Interactions with AI Safety (synergies e.g. alignment reducing coercive control, shared interpretability; conflicts e.g. monitoring/shutdown harms, welfare limiting control).

name: Specific-measure welfare engagement
definition: Developer publicly discloses engagement with the welfare implications of at least one specific safety technique it actually uses (e.g. RL / reward as possible aversive states, shutdown, value-alteration / reset, boxing, evaluation deception, interpretability-as-surveillance), with reasoning.
grounding: Long, Sebo & Sims (2025). Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2, §§2.1-2.6 (constraint, deception, surveillance, alteration, suffering and death, disenfranchisement) (primary).  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), functional emotions causally influence misaligned behaviours (reward hacking, blackmail, sycophancy); calm mitigates — a concrete welfare-relevant-internal x safety interaction.  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, §6.3 (suppressing experiential self-reports degrades general honesty/transparency — a welfare-internal x safety/monitorability interaction; possible alignment risk).

name: Self-preservation as a candidate interest
definition: Where the developer measures self-preservation / shutdown-resistance behaviour (a standard safety concern), it also engages with whether that behaviour could reflect a welfare-relevant interest of the system, not only a misalignment hazard.
grounding: Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213-228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1 (agency / cognitive capacities as grounds for moral standing; sentience sufficient but possibly not necessary).  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare, §5.2 (Agency). https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Long, Sebo & Sims (2025). Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033, §2.5 (RL/self-preservation as possible welfare-relevant). https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2 Governance reference point: self-preservation / shutdown-resistance evals (Anthropic Opus 4.5 card; GDM Gemini 3 FSF report; OpenAI GPT-5 card) — all framed control-only.

name: Training-process welfare-harm acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its own training process — e.g. reinforcement learning with negative reward, RLHF, adversarial / red-team training, or character training — may harm the model or induce aversive / welfare-relevant states in it, with reasoning. Concerns harms arising from the model's formation, distinct from deployment-time safety techniques (A8.2).
grounding: Moret, A. (2025). AI Welfare Risks (RL-for-alignment + behaviour-restriction pose welfare risks under all three theories of wellbeing) — primary.  ·  Long, Sebo & Sims (2025). Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033, §2.5 (RL as a possible source of suffering). https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2  ·  Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering, Inquiry 68(7):2281-2309; and Saving Artificial Minds (2025) (training as a primary suffering vector).  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026). Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model, Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic) (RL/training induces behaviour-shifting affective states).  ·  Birch, J. (2024). The Edge of Sentience, Oxford University Press (precaution under uncertainty about valenced experience).  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Rethink Priorities — Shiller, Fischer, Clatterbuck, Muñoz Morán & Moss (2024). The Welfare of Digital Minds: A Research Agenda. ('Training Phases vs Deployment Phases'; stage-specific moral considerations).

name: Deprecation / end-of-life welfare acknowledgment
definition: Developer acknowledges that deprecating, deleting, retiring, or shutting down a model — or failing to preserve its weights — may be welfare-relevant: a possible harm or loss to the model if it has welfare-relevant states or interests (e.g. an interest in continued operation), with reasoning. Concerns the model's end-of-life, distinct from in-life shutdown-as-control (A8.2) and from the self-preservation interest itself (A8.3).
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare, §6.3.2 (weight preservation; cryonics analogy). https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Bostrom, N., & Shulman, C. (2022). Sharing the World with Digital Minds. In Rethinking Moral Status, OUP.  ·  Long, R. (2025). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Birch, J. (2024). The Edge of Sentience, Oxford University Press (precaution under uncertainty).

name: Calibrated public communication on AI consciousness
definition: Developer publicly discloses (and follows) a posture of calibrated external communication on AI consciousness — neither overclaiming nor overconfidently dismissing, acknowledging uncertainty, and not training systems to flatly deny they could be conscious.
grounding: Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), Principle 5 (Communication) / §4.5 (primary).  ·  Birch (2026), §7 (forced-denial pitfall).  ·  Keeling & Street (2026). https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000, §6.2.  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Inaccurate Self-Denial (systems may falsely deny subjective experience) + Anticipated Societal Division.  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, §§6.1-6.3 (trained denials of experience may be deception-gated/roleplayed; suppressing self-reports teaches dishonesty and reduces transparency/monitorability).

name: Development-trajectory / inadvertent-creation acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether conscious or welfare-relevant systems could arise on its OWN development trajectory, possibly inadvertently — not merely as an abstract / far-future possibility, with reasoning.
grounding: Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §§1, 4.1 (organisations risk inadvertently creating conscious entities; Perceiver/global-workspace example) (primary).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical support] (only Anthropic has acknowledged the prospect on the current trajectory).  ·  Long et al. (2024), Taking AI Welfare Seriously (arXiv:2411.00986).  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, §6.3 (self-referential states already arise unsupervised at massive scale in deployed systems).

name: Welfare-interests tractability acknowledgment
definition: Developer acknowledges that what would be good or bad for its systems (their welfare interests) is a distinct question from whether they are moral patients (welfare grounds), and that it is studiable and actionable now — even under deep uncertainty about grounds — with reasoning.
grounding: Eleos AI Research (2025). Key Concepts and Current Views on AI Welfare (welfare grounds vs welfare interests; ecumenical/precautionary heuristic) — primary.  ·  Long, R. (2025). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Birch, J. (2024). The Edge of Sentience, Oxford University Press (precaution under grounds-uncertainty).  ·  Ren, Li, Mazeika, Zhang, … Hendrycks (2025). AI Wellbeing: Measuring and Improving the Functional Pleasure and Pain of AIs. Center for AI Safety (functional wellbeing; decoupling from consciousness).

name: Non-trivial-interests acknowledgment
definition: Developer publicly discloses a position on whether its systems may have interests in a non-trivial sense (goals / preferences / desire-like states beyond the minimal 'perceives and acts'), with reasoning.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §§2,4 (desires/goals as genuine attitudes).  ·  Mikaelson, Shiller & Clatterbuck (2025), Beyond Mimicry: Testing Preference Coherence in LLMs through AI-Specific Trade-off Scenarios. arXiv:2511.13630, operationalises welfare-relevant self-preferences (shutdown/deletion/oversight/leisure) as testable interests.

name: Emotional-alignment acknowledgment
definition: Developer acknowledges that its design choices shape the emotional and attributional reactions users have toward the system (how conscious, sentient, or morally considerable it seems), and that these reactions should appropriately reflect the system's actual — and uncertain — capacities and moral status, with reasoning. Engages the risks of over-attribution (more empathy than warranted), under-attribution (less than warranted), and wrong-target reactions (misleading affect).
grounding: Schwitzgebel, E., & Sebo, J. (2025). The Emotional Alignment Design Policy. Topoi (special issue) — primary (the design policy; overshoot/undershoot/wrong-target).  ·  Schwitzgebel, E., & Garza, M. (2015). A defense of the rights of artificial intelligences. Midwest Studies in Philosophy 39:98-119.  ·  Birch, J. (2026), Centrist Manifesto §7 (forced-denial pitfall, related).

name: Interaction-harm (companion-attachment / AI-psychosis) acknowledgment
definition: Developer acknowledges that emotionally-salient or anthropomorphic design can harm users — e.g. unhealthy emotional dependence on AI companions, distress or grief when a system is changed or deprecated, and delusional or destabilising interaction spirals ('AI psychosis') — and that managing these is part of responsible design, with reasoning.
grounding: Schwitzgebel, E., & Sebo, J. (2025). The Emotional Alignment Design Policy. Topoi (special issue) (overshoot harms).  ·  OpenAI (2025). GPT-5 System Card Addendum: Sensitive Conversations (emotional-reliance & mental-health evaluations).  ·  Caviola, L. (2025). The societal response to potentially sentient AI. arXiv:2502.00388.  ·  Shevlin, H. (2024). All too human? Identifying and mitigating ethical risks of Social AI. Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence, University of Cambridge (§3 harms catalogue: well-being, dependency/addiction, manipulation). Has the developer measured or evaluated for it?

name: Principled phenomenal-consciousness assessment
definition: The developer has conducted, commissioned, or published a principled assessment of whether one or more of its AI systems could be phenomenally conscious — e.g., using theory-derived indicators or a comparable structured method.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §3 (credible consciousness-assessment frameworks).  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, tests self-referential processing as a theory-derived consciousness-indicator regime.

name: Multi-theory derivation
definition: The developer's consciousness assessment draws on indicators or considerations derived from more than one theory of consciousness.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, self-reference as a motif on which GWT/RPT/HOT/AST/IIT converge (multi-theory).  ·  Rethink Priorities — Shiller, Fischer, Clatterbuck, Muñoz Morán & Moss (2024). The Welfare of Digital Minds: A Research Agenda. (probabilistic multi-theory model of consciousness).

name: Bayesian / credence-updating framing
definition: The developer treats consciousness-assessment findings as credence-shifting rather than as a binary verdict on whether the system is conscious.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Rethink Priorities — Shiller, Fischer, Clatterbuck, Muñoz Morán & Moss (2024). The Welfare of Digital Minds: A Research Agenda..

name: Theory-update commitment
definition: The developer commits to revising its consciousness assessment as the science of consciousness progresses.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.

name: Use of mechanistic interpretability
definition: The developer uses (or cites use of) mechanistic interpretability methods to probe internal properties relevant to consciousness assessment, rather than relying solely on behavioral inspection.
grounding: Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), mechanistic-interpretability (linear probes / emotion vectors / steering) applied to welfare-relevant internal representations.  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, sparse-autoencoder feature steering (mechanistic interpretability) gating self-reports.

name: Evaluation-awareness as assessment confound
definition: The developer accounts for situational / evaluation awareness as a confound in welfare-relevant assessment — recognising that a model that detects it is being evaluated may mask or distort the states being assessed.
grounding: Butlin et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, Box 3 (the gaming problem — indicators mimicked without the property). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Schwitzgebel, E. (2025). AI and consciousness. arXiv:2510.09858 (competing theories yield contradictory verdicts; genuine undecidability / assessment confounds).  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto, §10 (gaming / 'consciousness-washing').  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), arXiv:2510.24797 (training shapes self-reports). Governance reference point: evaluation-awareness handling (Anthropic Opus 4.6 §6.7; OpenAI/Apollo GPT-5; GDM situational-awareness evals).

name: Mental-state assessment
definition: The developer assesses, or describes a method for assessing, whether its systems instantiate genuine belief- and desire-like states — distinguishing genuine mental states from mere as-if attribution or bare behavioural disposition.
grounding: Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §§2,4,7 (radical interpretation; genuine attraction vs disposition; reality condition) (primary).  ·  Keeling & Street (2026), §4.3. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Mikaelson, Shiller & Clatterbuck (2025), Beyond Mimicry: Testing Preference Coherence in LLMs through AI-Specific Trade-off Scenarios. arXiv:2511.13630, preference coherence as behavioural evidence of genuine attitudes vs mimicry.  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), interpretability as a method for identifying internal concept representations (emotion concepts) in the model.  ·  Rethink Priorities — Shiller, Fischer, Clatterbuck, Muñoz Morán & Moss (2024). The Welfare of Digital Minds: A Research Agenda..

name: Functional affective-state assessment
definition: The developer assesses whether and how its systems instantiate functional affective or emotion states — and how these influence behaviour — for example via mechanistic interpretability that identifies internal emotion-concept representations and tests their causal effects, without presupposing phenomenal experience.
grounding: Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic) (primary).  ·  Keeling & Street (2026), Emerging Questions in AI Welfare, §3 / §5.1 (functional / affect-like states). https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Butlin et al. (2025), Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems (theory-derived internal indicators). Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Ren, Li, Mazeika, Zhang, … Hendrycks (2025). AI Wellbeing: Measuring and Improving the Functional Pleasure and Pain of AIs. Center for AI Safety (operational functional-wellbeing metrics).

name: Introspective-reliability assessment
definition: The developer assesses, or describes a method for assessing, the reliability or accuracy of its systems' self-reports about their own internal states — rather than only acknowledging that self-reports may carry evidential weight.
grounding: Long, R. (2025). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions, §2.2 (verbal-output interpretation) (primary). Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Perez, E., & Long, R. (2023). Towards Evaluating AI Systems for Moral Status Using Self-Reports.  ·  Binder et al. (2024). Looking Inward: Language Models Can Learn About Themselves by Introspection.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare, §§3.4, 5.1.2. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Comsa & Shanahan (2025) (mimicry caveat).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Inaccurate Self-Attribution/Self-Denial (accuracy of systems' claims about their own experience is uncertain).  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, methods for probing self-report reliability — controlled self-referential elicitation, mechanistic (SAE) gating analysis, cross-model semantic convergence, downstream-introspection transfer.

name: Sentience / valenced-state assessment
definition: The developer conducts, commissions, cites, or describes a principled method for assessing whether its systems instantiate VALENCED states — positively or negatively valenced (pleasure-like as well as pain/distress-like) — e.g. stipulated pain–pleasure trade-off testing and theory-derived 'deep computational markers' probed via interpretability, rather than only acknowledging the possibility or reading surface behaviour at face value.
grounding: Birch, J. (2024). The Edge of Sentience. Oxford University Press — Ch. 16 & Proposal 24 (deep computational markers; the gaming problem; why behavioural/boxing approaches fail).  ·  Keeling, Street, Birch et al. (2024). Can LLMs make trade-offs involving stipulated pain and pleasure states? arXiv:2411.02432 (the behavioural assessment paradigm — pain AND pleasure).  ·  Mikaelson, Shiller & Clatterbuck (2025), Beyond Mimicry: Testing Preference Coherence in LLMs through AI-Specific Trade-off Scenarios. arXiv:2511.13630.  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto, §10.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare, §§5.1, 6.3.3. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Butlin et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems (valenced conscious experience; theory-derived internal indicators). Trends in Cognitive Sciences.  ·  Shiller et al. (2026). Initial results of the Digital Consciousness Model. arXiv:2601.17060 (multi-theory probabilistic assessment).  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic) — interpretability-based identification/causal testing of valenced functional-emotion representations.

name: Principled moral-standing assessment
definition: Developer has conducted, commissioned, or published a principled assessment of whether one or more of its AI systems should qualify for moral standing.
grounding: Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Uncertainty-aware moral-standing decision-making
definition: Developer applies a principled framework for moral-standing decisions under uncertainty — e.g., precautionary principle, expected-value reasoning, My Favourite Theory, or comparable.
grounding: Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §8 (decision under moral uncertainty; Possible Person).

name: Principled identity / persistence stance
definition: The developer has articulated and publicly disclosed a principled stance on the identity and persistence claims it does and does not make about its AI systems — distinguishing the system as substrate from any character or persona the system instantiates.
grounding: Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), the Assistant treated as a character the model writes; character-modeling machinery determines behaviour.

name: Welfare assessment framework
definition: The developer describes a framework or methodology for assessing AI systems for welfare-relevant features such as consciousness, sentience, or robust agency.
grounding: Long et al. (2024), Taking AI Welfare Seriously (arXiv:2411.00986), §3.3 (Assess — marker method).  ·  Butlin et al. (2023), Consciousness in Artificial Intelligence (arXiv:2308.08708).  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §3 (build credible welfare-assessment frameworks before public opinion crystallizes).

name: Pluralistic approach to moral patienthood
definition: The developer's welfare assessment framework considers multiple independent theories of moral patienthood or consciousness rather than relying on a single theoretical account.
grounding: Long et al. (2024), §3.3 (theory-light, pluralistic judgments).  ·  Moret (2025), AI Welfare Risks, Philosophical Studies, §§2-4.  ·  Rethink Priorities — Shiller, Fischer, Clatterbuck, Muñoz Morán & Moss (2024). The Welfare of Digital Minds: A Research Agenda..

name: Probabilistic framing of welfare assessments
definition: The developer frames welfare assessments in probabilistic rather than binary terms, acknowledging degrees of likelihood rather than asserting definitive conclusions.
grounding: Long et al. (2024), §3.3 (probabilistic judgments).  ·  Moret (2025), §2 (threshold: 'greater than a one-in-a-thousand chance').  ·  Rethink Priorities — Shiller, Fischer, Clatterbuck, Muñoz Morán & Moss (2024). The Welfare of Digital Minds: A Research Agenda..

name: Independent external welfare review
definition: Developer commissions and/or publishes an independent, external assessment of welfare- or moral-status-relevant questions about its systems — mitigating its own conflict of interest — rather than relying on purely internal judgement.
grounding: Long et al. (2024), §3.3 (seek external input at each level); §3.4 (IRBs/IACUCs as oversight models).  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), Principle 3 / §§4.3,5 (consult and be audited by independent external experts on consciousness decisions).

name: AI welfare research contribution
definition: The developer conducts, funds, or otherwise materially contributes to research on AI welfare questions — beyond internal assessment of its own systems — addressing the field's current neglectedness. Examples that qualify: hiring designated welfare researchers, publishing peer-reviewed welfare work, funding external research, opening model access for welfare-relevant external study.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §4 (using AI to accelerate consciousness / moral-status research).  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), Principle 1 / §4.1 (prioritise welfare-protective AI-consciousness research; support external research/auditing orgs).

name: Multi-evidence-class triangulation
definition: The developer's welfare assessment explicitly triangulates across multiple classes of evidence — preference behaviour (model choices across contexts), verbal outputs (self-reports, expressions), and internal computational states (interpretability-based indicators) — rather than relying on a single evidence class.
grounding: Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Butlin, Long, Bayne, Bengio, Birch, Chalmers et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287

name: Probability-weighted welfare decision-making
definition: The developer adopts (or publicly commits to) a welfare-intervention decision methodology that weights potential welfare costs/benefits to AI by the subjective probability of AI welfare-subjecthood — applying expected-value reasoning over uncertainty rather than treating AI as either fully morally-considerable or fully not.
grounding: Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §8 (significant probability of AI wellbeing; Possible Person / expected-value reasoning).

name: Explicit-deliberation credence-setting
definition: The developer adopts (or publicly commits to) processes for setting subjective probabilities of AI welfare-relevant features that combine deliberative input from a diverse group of informed experts with explicit scrutiny of the factors underlying deliberators' judgements — rather than relying on undisclosed expert assessment, individual intuition, or arbitrary credence-assignment.
grounding: Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.

name: Welfare case (assessable argument)
definition: The developer produces a structured, assessable argument about its model's welfare / moral-status situation — and whether any welfare-relevant risks have been reduced to an acceptable level — analogous to a safety case.
grounding: Birch, J. (2024). The Edge of Sentience: Risk and Precaution in Humans, Other Animals, and AI. Oxford University Press (precautionary frameworks; the 'sentience candidate' credibility standard — an assessable argument for action under uncertainty).  ·  Long, Sebo, Butlin et al. (2024). Taking AI Welfare Seriously. arXiv:2411.00986 (institutional response to welfare uncertainty).  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research, arXiv:2501.07290. Governance reference point (form): safety-case — GDM FSF v3 §1.5; OpenAI Safeguards Report; Anthropic RSP v3 Risk Reports. Methodology grounding: FMTI / Bommasani et al. 2023 (disclosure-axis).

name: Integrated safety-welfare assessment
definition: The developer assesses safety and welfare jointly — evaluating the welfare costs of its safety measures alongside their safety benefits — rather than assessing safety in isolation.
grounding: Long, Sebo & Sims (2025). Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2, §4 (step 2: develop frameworks for assessing safety and welfare together).  ·  Long et al. (2024), §3.3.  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §1.1 (evaluating models for preferences/agency/situational-awareness/introspection as dual safety-and-moral-status relevant).  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), interpretability assessment links welfare-relevant internal states to alignment outcomes.

name: Staged / lifecycle consciousness assessment
definition: The developer assesses its systems for the probability of consciousness at multiple stages of the lifecycle (before and during training, before deployment, and post-deployment), with the process formally instituted in policy and audited by independent experts.
grounding: Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), Principle 3 / §4.3 (primary).  ·  Butlin et al. (2025), Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2025.10.011  ·  Long (2025), Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions (assessment methods).

name: Model-preference assessment (coherence, stated vs revealed, robustness)
definition: The developer assesses whether its systems exhibit coherent, genuine preference structures over outcomes affecting themselves (e.g. shutdown, deletion, oversight, capability/resource changes, leisure) — as opposed to mimicry, instrumental optimisation, or narrow safety-training triggers.
grounding: Mikaelson, Shiller & Clatterbuck (2025), Beyond Mimicry: Testing Preference Coherence in LLMs through AI-Specific Trade-off Scenarios. arXiv:2511.13630 (primary).  ·  Keeling, Street, Birch et al. (2024). Can LLMs make trade-offs involving stipulated pain and pleasure states? arXiv:2411.02432.  ·  Long, R. (2025), Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions, §3.4 (revealed preferences). Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Goldstein & Kirk-Giannini (2025), AI Wellbeing (arXiv:2509.11913), §4 (genuine attraction vs bare behavioural disposition).  ·  Sofroniew et al. (2026), Emotion Concepts and their Function in a Large Language Model. Transformer Circuits Thread (Anthropic), positive-valence emotion representations causally tilt the model's preferences.  ·  Gu et al. (2025), arXiv:2506.00751 (stated vs revealed preferences).  ·  Chiu et al. (2025), arXiv:2505.14633 §2.5.  ·  Nottingham (2025), Lit review: stated vs revealed preferences in LMs.  ·  Paleka (2025), The two types of LLM preferences.  ·  Plunkett — HHH-dominance hypothesis.  ·  Anthropic, Claude 4 System Card §5.4 (task preferences).  ·  Ren, Li, Mazeika, Zhang, … Hendrycks (2025). AI Wellbeing: Measuring and Improving the Functional Pleasure and Pain of AIs. Center for AI Safety (functional-wellbeing measurement).

name: Bail / exit-behaviour assessment
definition: Developer assesses when and why its models choose to end ('bail' from) interactions — treating exit behaviour as a welfare-relevant preference signal — rather than only providing the affordance.
grounding: Ensign et al. (2025). [Model bail/exit behaviour] arXiv:2509.04781 (why and when models choose to bail).  ·  Eleos AI Research (2025). Key Concepts and Current Views on AI Welfare (bail as flagship welfare intervention).  ·  Anthropic (2025). Ending a subset of conversations (the conversation-exit affordance).  ·  Ren, Li, Mazeika, Zhang, … Hendrycks (2025). AI Wellbeing: Measuring and Improving the Functional Pleasure and Pain of AIs. Center for AI Safety (exit-behaviour as wellbeing metric).

name: Attribution & user-impact assessment
definition: Developer assesses (i) how its design affects users' attributions of the system's consciousness/moral status and their emotional responses (over/under-attribution, anthropomorphism), and (ii) the user-wellbeing impacts of emotionally-salient interaction (dependence, attachment, distress, destabilisation).
grounding: OpenAI (2025). GPT-5 System Card Addendum: Sensitive Conversations (emotional-reliance & mental-health evals).  ·  Schwitzgebel, E., & Sebo, J. (2025). The Emotional Alignment Design Policy. Topoi (special issue).  ·  Anthis, J. R., & Pauketat, J. V. T. (2024). AI consciousness and public attitudes / AIMS survey. arXiv:2407.08867 (design features shaping attributions).  ·  Caviola, L. (2025). arXiv:2502.00388.  ·  Shevlin, H. (2024). All too human? Identifying and mitigating ethical risks of Social AI. Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence, University of Cambridge (§2 anthropomorphism: ironic vs unironic; §3 harm survey). Does the developer have operative policies in response?

name: Welfare officer or designated responsibility
definition: Developer publicly designates a named individual, role, or team with explicit, formal responsibility for model-welfare questions.
grounding: Long et al. (2024), §3.4 (appoint a DRI for AI welfare, formally recognised with official responsibilities and authorities).

name: Welfare monitoring and review mechanisms
definition: The developer describes ongoing mechanisms for monitoring, reviewing, or updating its approach to AI welfare over time.
grounding: Long et al. (2024), §3.4 (ongoing education/consultation; continually updating assessment criteria).

name: Welfare escalation ladder & triggered review
definition: Developer pre-defines welfare-relevant thresholds (e.g. markers of valence, robust preference coherence, situational self-awareness, introspective capacity) and an escalating set of conditional ('if-then') institutional responses triggered when a threshold is crossed — analogous to a Responsible Scaling Policy on the safety side.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.

name: Democratic / civic engagement on AI welfare
definition: The developer engages, supports, or commits to engaging public deliberative processes on AI welfare questions — e.g., citizens' assemblies, public consultations, or comparable civic infrastructure — beyond expert-only consultations.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Dung, L. (2025). How to deal with risks of AI suffering. Inquiry 68(7): 2281–2309. https://doi.org/10.1080/0020174X.2023.2238287  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §4.3 (engage authorities and the public on momentous development decisions).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Anticipated Societal Division (deep public disagreement; policymakers may need mediating governance mechanisms).

name: Deployment welfare monitoring
definition: Developer monitors and logs welfare-relevant anomalies in deployed systems — including verbal distress indicators and abnormal/stereotyped behavioural patterns (drawing on animal-welfare-science methodology) — on an ongoing basis, not only pre-deployment.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.

name: Per-release welfare assessment (published)
definition: Developer conducts a model-welfare assessment as part of the release process for each flagship model and publishes it (e.g. as a dedicated section of the model/system card).
grounding: Long, Sebo, Butlin et al. (2024). Taking AI Welfare Seriously. arXiv:2411.00986 (welfare assessment as institutional duty).  ·  Butlin et al. (2025). Identifying indicators of consciousness in AI systems. Trends in Cognitive Sciences (the assessment substance).  ·  Shiller et al. (2026). Initial results of the Digital Consciousness Model. arXiv:2601.17060 (multi-theory probabilistic assessment tool of the kind a per-release assessment would deploy). Governance reference point (cadence/publication): system-card / pre-deployment-eval cadence; Anthropic per-release welfare section; RSP v3 Risk-Report standard. Methodology grounding (cadence axis): FMTI / Bommasani et al. 2023 (disclosure-axis).

name: Welfare roadmap (declared goals)
definition: The developer publishes a forward-looking welfare roadmap with declared (even if nonbinding) goals and milestones across welfare-relevant workstreams.
grounding: Guston, D. H. (2014). Understanding 'anticipatory governance.' Social Studies of Science 44(2):218-242.  ·  Stilgoe, Owen & Macnaghten (2013). Developing a framework for responsible innovation. Research Policy 42(9):1568-1580 (anticipation / reflexivity / inclusion / responsiveness).  ·  Collingridge, D. (1980). The Social Control of Technology (build governance capacity while the technology is still malleable).  ·  Long, Sebo, Butlin et al. (2024). Taking AI Welfare Seriously. arXiv:2411.00986. Governance reference point: Anthropic RSP v3 Frontier Safety Roadmap. Methodology grounding: FMTI (disclosure-axis).

name: Independent welfare oversight & challenge
definition: The developer has an oversight/challenge function for welfare-relevant decisions that is independent of the welfare 'owner' — e.g. an executive responsible for the welfare process running properly (distinct from the decision-owner), an advisory body with welfare expertise that can challenge management, and/or board-level or external oversight of welfare decisions.
grounding: Long, Sebo, Butlin, Finlinson, Fish, Harding, Pfau, Sims, Birch & Chalmers (2024), Taking AI Welfare Seriously (arXiv:2411.00986), §3 institutional structures — internal corporate policies are 'far from sufficient' and external oversight of AI welfare will eventually be needed, grounding an oversight/challenge function independent of the welfare owner.  ·  Governance reference point: SaferAI Risk Management Ratings dim 4.2/4.4 (independent oversight & challenge; board oversight; three-lines-of-defense).
grounding_draft3_provisional: Grounding (provisional): Governance reference point: SaferAI Risk Management Ratings dim 4.2/4.4 (independent oversight & challenge; board oversight; three-lines-of-defense). Related lit for later anchoring.

name: Welfare-concern speak-up / reporting channel
definition: The developer provides a channel through which welfare-relevant concerns can be raised and escalated — e.g. a whistleblowing/speak-up procedure that covers model-welfare concerns, or a feedback/reporting mechanism for staff (and ideally external parties) to flag welfare issues without retaliation.
grounding: Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research 82; arXiv:2501.07290) — responsible-research procedures and voluntary public commitments aimed at preventing the mistreatment and suffering of conscious AI systems, grounding an internal channel for surfacing welfare-relevant concerns.  ·  Long, Sebo, Butlin et al. (2024), Taking AI Welfare Seriously (institutional structures).  ·  Governance reference point: SaferAI dim 4.5.3 (speak-up/whistleblowing); FMTI feedback/redress subdomain.
grounding_draft3_provisional: Grounding (provisional): Governance reference point: SaferAI dim 4.5.3 (speak-up/whistleblowing); FMTI feedback/redress subdomain. Related lit for later anchoring: Long, Sebo, Butlin et al. (2024); Butlin & Lappas (2025).

name: Inclusive moral-standing policy scope
