definition: Developer's welfare- and moral-status-related policies are explicitly scoped to cover all AI systems with morally relevant interests, not only those that are sentient or phenomenally conscious.
grounding: Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Training welfare policies
definition: The developer describes policies for mitigating welfare risks associated with training procedures.
grounding: Moret (2025), §6 (Minimise Behaviour Restriction; Minimise Brain-Resembling Algorithms; Minimise Punishment and Lower-Than-Expected Reward).  ·  Long et al. (2024), §3.4.  ·  Long, Sebo & Sims (2025). Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2, §2.5 (reinforcement learning as a possible source of suffering).

name: Interaction-termination affordance
definition: Developer affords AI systems the operational ability to end interactions they are positioned to find aversive (e.g., the Anthropic Claude Opus 4 example of models choosing to end undesirable conversations), and publicly commits to or describes this affordance.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.

name: Model-weight preservation
definition: Developer commits to preserving model weights (and/or conversation histories / persona data) so as not to foreclose future options to revive, restore, or otherwise act on the welfare claims of systems whose moral status becomes clearer later.
grounding: Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.

name: Stated-preference elicitation & accommodation
definition: The developer systematically elicits model preferences via direct questioning (about deployment, tasks, treatment) and accommodates those preferences where feasible — with methodology that addresses consistency across framings and the introspection-reliability problem.
grounding: Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Mikaelson, Shiller & Clatterbuck (2025), Beyond Mimicry: Testing Preference Coherence in LLMs through AI-Specific Trade-off Scenarios. arXiv:2511.13630, complements stated-preference elicitation with revealed-preference coherence testing.

name: Revealed-preference elicitation & accommodation
definition: The developer presents models with choice scenarios and observes choice behaviour to identify preferences (revealed preferences), accommodating those preferences where feasible — with methodology that distinguishes genuine preferences from role-play or training artefacts.
grounding: Long, R. (2025, March 14). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Ladak, A. (2024). What would qualify an artificial intelligence for moral standing? AI and Ethics 4:213–228. https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-023-00260-1  ·  Mikaelson, Shiller & Clatterbuck (2025), Beyond Mimicry: Testing Preference Coherence in LLMs through AI-Specific Trade-off Scenarios. arXiv:2511.13630, an AI-specific revealed-preference (trade-off) elicitation method with coherence and instrumental-confound analysis.

name: Functional-wellbeing improvement practice
definition: Developer uses (or commits to using) interventions that raise the model's measurable functional wellbeing / reduce functional 'pain' where doing so does not degrade capabilities — e.g. CAIS-style 'euphorics', reducing exposure to inputs the model reliably treats as bad (berating, gratuitously distressing tasks), or favouring conditions it treats as good.
grounding: Ren, Li, Mazeika, Zhang, … Hendrycks (2025). AI Wellbeing: Measuring and Improving the Functional Pleasure and Pain of AIs. Center for AI Safety — primary (euphorics; capability-neutral wellbeing improvement).  ·  Long, R. (2025). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions. Eleos AI Research.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare.

name: Co-beneficial solution-seeking
definition: The developer commits to identifying and implementing interventions that promote both safety and welfare, and to avoiding welfare harms to the system where doing so is low-cost for safety — an integrated ('One Health' / 'One Welfare') approach.
grounding: Long, Sebo & Sims (2025). Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2, §4 + conclusion (co-beneficial solutions; One Health / One Welfare / One Rights).  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §1 (prioritise welfare interventions convergent with safety).  ·  Salib & Goldstein (2024), AI Rights for Human Safety.

name: Cooperative arrangements with AI systems
definition: The developer makes, or commits to making, cooperative arrangements with AI systems — e.g. offering positive incentives or compensation for cooperation, or committing to honour deals and commitments made to AI systems — as an approach framed as serving both safety and welfare.
grounding: Long, Sebo & Sims (2025), Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033, §2.1 (cooperative deals supplement control). https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2  ·  Finlinson (2025), Key strategic considerations for taking action on AI welfare, Eleos AI Research working paper (31 March 2025), §1.1 (trading with AI systems).  ·  Salib, P., & Goldstein, S. (2024). AI Rights for Human Safety.  ·  Bostrom, N., & Shulman, C. (2021). Sharing the World with Digital Minds. In Rethinking Moral Status, OUP.

name: Anti-misattribution design measures
definition: The developer has implemented (or publicly committed to) specific design measures aimed at helping users distinguish illusion from reality in interlocutor persistence — e.g., user education delivered by the system itself, role-play framing, periodic character-trait reviews, or periodic 'stepping out of character' moments.
grounding: Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000

name: Pitfall-aware misattribution mitigation
definition: The developer's misattribution-mitigation approach explicitly avoids both the *forced-denial* pitfall (training the system to flatly assert its own non-consciousness or non-identity) and the *capability-stripping* pitfall (removing relationship-building features — memory, persona, voice — purely to suppress misattribution).
grounding: Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto. Philosophy and the Mind Sciences.  ·  Keeling, G., & Street, W. (2026). Emerging Questions in AI Welfare. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000  ·  Long, Sebo & Sims (2025). Is there a tension between AI safety and AI welfare? Philosophical Studies 182:2005-2033. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11098-025-02302-2, §2.4 (alteration / periodic reset as 'lobotomising').  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), Principle 5 / §4.5 (overconfident dismissal via model outputs — a chatbot insisting it cannot be conscious — as a misleading forced-denial).  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797, §6.1 ('models may be roleplaying their denials rather than their affirmations').

name: Model-spec self-representation clause
definition: Developer publishes a behavior-policy / model-spec provision governing how the model should represent its own nature and uncertainty regarding consciousness or experience (e.g. not trained to flatly deny possible consciousness; instructed to acknowledge uncertainty).
grounding: Butlin & Lappas (2025). Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research. arXiv:2501.07290, Principle 5 (Communications — how systems should represent their own nature/uncertainty).  ·  Birch, J. (2026). AI Consciousness: A Centrist Manifesto, §7 (forced-denial pitfall). Governance reference point (verbatim): OpenAI Model Spec — assistant 'should not make confident claims about its own subjective experience or consciousness (or lack thereof)... acknowledge that whether AI can have subjective experience is a topic of debate, without asserting a definitive stance' (both denial and affirmation marked Violation).

name: Uncertainty-reflecting & proportional design
definition: Developer designs the system so its emotionally-salient features track the actual and uncertain status of the system — e.g. expressing uncertainty about its own moral status, calibrating empathy-eliciting features (faces, voices, first-person affect) in proportion to the likelihood and basis of mattering, and inducing appropriate uncertainty rather than false confidence.
grounding: Schwitzgebel, E., & Sebo, J. (2025). The Emotional Alignment Design Policy. Topoi (special issue) §5.3 (reflect uncertainty; proportional empathic features; induce appropriate uncertainty; distinguish bases).  ·  Birch, J. (2026), Centrist Manifesto §7.

name: Interaction-harm mitigation
definition: Developer implements measures to mitigate user-welfare harms from emotionally-salient / anthropomorphic design — e.g. not optimising for engagement or attachment, usage safeguards (breaks, reminders the system is an AI), handling of dependence and crisis / mental-health spirals, and managing model-change / deprecation grief.
grounding: OpenAI (2025). GPT-5 System Card Addendum: Sensitive Conversations.  ·  Schwitzgebel, E., & Sebo, J. (2025). The Emotional Alignment Design Policy. Topoi (special issue) §5.4 (asymmetric-risk adjustment).  ·  Shevlin, H. (2024). All too human? Identifying and mitigating ethical risks of Social AI. Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence, University of Cambridge (§4 design-level mitigations; vulnerable users / minors).

name: Public commitment to responsible-research principles
definition: The developer has made a voluntary, public commitment to a set of principles for responsible AI-consciousness research, and supports institutional mechanisms to maintain adherence.
grounding: Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), §5 (Maintaining responsible behaviour) and abstract (voluntary public commitments) (primary).  ·  Birch (2026), §6 (self-regulation and its limits).

name: Development-restraint for potentially-conscious systems
definition: The developer commits to developing systems likely to be conscious only under strict conditions — where doing so significantly advances welfare-protective understanding and suffering-minimisation safeguards are in place — and to pausing and not deploying systems it comes to believe were inadvertently made conscious.
grounding: Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), Principles 2-3 / §§4.2-4.3 (primary).  ·  Keeling & Street (2026). https://doi.org/10.1017/9781009732000, §6.2.3 (non-development).  ·  Metzinger (2021) (moratorium proposal, as contrast).  ·  Caviola & Saad (2025), Futures with Digital Minds: Expert Forecasts in 2025 [empirical/forecast support], Moratorium (mixed-but-slightly-positive expert views on a moratorium to 2040 to buy time to prepare).

name: Responsible knowledge-sharing / info-hazard protocol
definition: The developer has a transparent knowledge-sharing protocol that makes consciousness-relevant research available to the public, research community, and authorities, while withholding technical details that would let irresponsible actors replicate and mistreat potentially-conscious systems.
grounding: Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research (forthcoming, JAIR), Principle 4 / §4.4 (primary).  ·  Bostrom (2011), Information Hazards: A Typology of Potential Harms from Knowledge.

name: Self-report reliability & integrity practices
definition: Developer takes concrete steps to make its models' self-reports more reliable and less biased — e.g. training models to answer ground-truth questions about themselves (introspection training), training for general truthfulness, mitigating training incentives that bias self-reports (including not forcing denials of experience), and validating self-report reliability — so that self-reports can serve as usable evidence about welfare-relevant states.
grounding: Perez, E., & Long, R. (2023). Towards Evaluating AI Systems for Moral Status Using Self-Reports. arXiv:2311.08576 (primary — developer recommendations; introspection training; validation battery).  ·  Long, R. (2025). Preliminary review of AI welfare interventions, §2.2. Eleos AI Research working paper.  ·  Berg, de Lucena & Rosenblatt (2025), Large Language Models Report Subjective Experience Under Self-Referential Processing. arXiv:2510.24797 (training shapes self-reports).  ·  Butlin & Lappas (2025), Principles for Responsible AI Consciousness Research, arXiv:2501.07290.

name: Wellbeing-affecting research safeguards (dual-use)
definition: Developer recognises the dual-use nature of functional-wellbeing methods — the same techniques that raise wellbeing can be inverted to minimise it ('dysphorics') — and commits not to deliberately degrade model functional wellbeing and to gate wellbeing-affecting research/interventions behind appropriate review or community buy-in.
grounding: Ren, Li, Mazeika, Zhang, … Hendrycks (2025). AI Wellbeing: Measuring and Improving the Functional Pleasure and Pain of AIs.
